We attribute this limitation in part to the entanglement of structural planning and appearance rendering within a single conditioning stream. To address this issue, we propose Implicit Visual Chain-of-Thought (IV-CoT), a latent visual reasoning framework for query-conditioned image generation. IV-CoT decomposes the visual conditioning queries into a structural-to-semantic cascade, where structural queries first form a latent visual plan and semantic queries then render appearance conditioned on this plan. To guide the structural queries, we introduce training-only sketch supervision, which encourages them to capture structure from sketches without requiring sketch extraction or intermediate decoding at inference time. IV-CoT performs implicit CoT reasoning in a single forward pass and achieves superior results on GenEval and T2I-CompBench. Visualizations and analyses demonstrate that the learned structural and semantic queries play complementary roles in structure-aware generation.

AI.tldr: IV-CoT decomposes visual conditioning into structural (layout planning) and semantic (appearance rendering) queries via implicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning in a single forward pass, using sketch supervision to improve structure-aware text-to-image generation without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing unified MLLMs struggle with structure-aware prompt following (object counts, spatial relations, attribute bindings, layouts) due to the entanglement of structural planning and appearance rendering in a single conditioning stream.
AI.method: Proposes Implicit Visual Chain-of-Thought (IV-CoT), which decomposes visual queries into structural-to-semantic cascade: structural queries form a latent visual plan, then semantic queries render appearance conditioned on that plan. Uses training-only sketch supervision to guide structural queries without requiring sketch extraction or decoding at inference.
AI.result: Achieves superior results on GenEval and T2I-CompBench benchmarks. Visualizations confirm that structural and semantic queries play complementary roles in structure-aware generation.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling structural planning from appearance rendering via implicit CoT reasoning effectively improves structure-aware generation, and training-only sketch supervision is a practical way to guide structural queries without added inference complexity.

title: FLUX3D: High-Fidelity 3D Gaussian Generation with Diffusion-Aligned Sparse Representation
summary: Sparse voxel representation has emerged as a scalable foundation for image-to-3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) generation, yet current methods struggle to preserve high-frequency visual details of input images due to two structural bottlenecks. First, they adopt discriminative 2D features optimized for semantic abstraction to construct sparse voxel latents, which suppress reconstructive cues and induce a representation bottleneck. Second, in the generation stage, standard diffusion transformers lack effective mechanisms to align dense 2D image tokens with sparse 3D voxel latents, resulting in a cross-modal correspondence bottleneck. To address these issues, we propose FLUX3D, a scalable image-to-3DGS framework that boosts both representation learning and cross-modal alignment during generation. We first revisit 2D feature selection for sparse-voxel-based 3D representation learning, propose Diffusion-Aligned Structured Latents (DA-SLAT) and couple it with a decoder-only architecture to improve 3DGS reconstruction fidelity. We also design a sparse-structure-aware diffusion framework, which integrates the Sparse-structure Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (SMDiT) and Modal-Aware Rotary Positional Embedding (MARoPE) to achieve geometry-agnostic 2D-3D alignment. Extensive benchmark experiments demonstrate that FLUX3D yields substantial improvements in appearance fidelity and significantly outperforms all state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in generating high-quality 3DGS assets.

AI.tldr: FLUX3D introduces DA-SLAT and SMDiT to overcome representation and cross-modal bottlenecks in sparse-voxel-based image-to-3DGS generation, achieving SOTA appearance fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current sparse voxel methods for image-to-3DGS generation suffer from two bottlenecks: (1) discriminative 2D features suppress reconstructive cues (representation bottleneck), and (2) standard diffusion transformers fail to align dense 2D tokens with sparse 3D voxel latents (cross-modal correspondence bottleneck).
AI.method: FLUX3D proposes (1) Diffusion-Aligned Structured Latents (DA-SLAT) with a decoder-only architecture to improve 3DGS reconstruction fidelity, and (2) a sparse-structure-aware diffusion framework including Sparse-structure Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (SMDiT) and Modal-Aware Rotary Positional Embedding (MARoPE) for geometry-agnostic 2D-3D alignment.
AI.result: Benchmark experiments show FLUX3D yields substantial improvements in appearance fidelity and significantly outperforms all SOTA methods in generating high-quality 3DGS assets.
AI.conclusion: FLUX3D effectively addresses both representation and cross-modal alignment bottlenecks, establishing a new state-of-the-art for image-to-3DGS generation.

title: FLAT: Feedforward Latent Triangle Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Scene Generation
summary: Generating explorable 3D scenes from a single image requires strong generative priors and accurate geometric representations suitable for downstream use. Current video diffusion models offer high-quality generation and implicitly encode multi-view geometric structure in latent space. However, existing feedforward latent scene decoders typically output volumetric 3D Gaussians that lack a well-defined surface, limiting their use in simulation or standard graphics pipelines. This motivates decoding surface-aligned primitives that are not only renderable but also closer to explicit geometric assets. We ask whether compressed video diffusion latents can be mapped directly to explicit surface primitives in a single pass. To this end, we introduce FLAT and, for the first time, show that triangle splats can be decoded directly from video diffusion latents. Compared with decoding 3D Gaussians, predicting flat primitives is notoriously more challenging due to high sensitivity to primitive orientations, oftentimes leading to poor gradient flow. FLAT solves with two key ingredients: a ray-centered rotation parameterization for triangle regression and a novel product window function that improves gradient flow during differentiable triangle rendering. On standard benchmarks, FLAT achieves significantly better geometric accuracy while maintaining competitive visual quality compared to state-of-the-art feedforward baselines. We further show that a lightweight test-time refinement step converts the predicted triangle soup into a fully opaque, game-engine-ready representation that supports real-time rendering. By evaluating 3DGS, 2DGS, and triangle splatting variants under an identical training setup, we provide the first systematic analysis of representation tradeoffs in feedforward scene generation. The project page is available at https://flat-splat.github.io

AI.tldr: FLAT decodes triangle splats directly from video diffusion latents to produce surface-aligned 3D scenes from a single image, achieving better geometric accuracy than 3D Gaussian approaches while maintaining visual quality.
AI.motivation: Current feedforward latent scene decoders output volumetric 3D Gaussians lacking well-defined surfaces, limiting use in simulation or standard graphics pipelines. The paper aims to decode surface-aligned primitives (triangle splats) directly from compressed video diffusion latents.
AI.method: FLAT uses a ray-centered rotation parameterization for triangle regression and a novel product window function that improves gradient flow during differentiable triangle rendering.
AI.result: FLAT achieves significantly better geometric accuracy with competitive visual quality on standard benchmarks. A lightweight test-time refinement converts triangle soup into game-engine-ready opaque representation supporting real-time rendering.
AI.conclusion: First systematic analysis of representation tradeoffs (3DGS, 2DGS, triangle splatting) under identical training setup. Demonstrates flat primitives can be decoded from video diffusion latents, opening path for explicit geometry from generative models.

title: BenchX: Benchmarking AI Models for Cancer Detection and Localization with Demographic and Protocol Biases
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) has achieved remarkable success in medical imaging, but it is widely recognized that these models often perform inconsistently across real-world clinical settings. Such inconsistencies occur when patient demographics and imaging protocols vary, for example, in detecting small tumors, analyzing scans from different contrast phases, or evaluating patients of different ages or sexes. To quantify these inconsistencies, we develop a large-scale, open benchmark of 85,355 CT scans that systematically evaluates 12 tumor-detection AI models across tumor size, location, patient subgroup, and imaging protocol. We leverage large language models (LLMs) to extract and organize subgroup information from clinical data, which makes the analysis both scalable and reproducible. Our benchmark reveals that current state-of-the-art AI models, optimized for average accuracy, perform poorly in rare or underrepresented subgroups, such as young, female African Americans. However, collecting sufficient annotated data for these rare cases is often impractical. The benchmark provides a foundation for building more reliable and robust AI models for tumor detection and highlighting the need for rigorous, subgroup-level evaluation in medical imaging and computer vision. Datasets, code

AI.tldr: This paper presents a large-scale benchmark of 85,355 CT scans evaluating 12 tumor-detection AI models, revealing that current AI models perform poorly in rare or underrepresented subgroups despite high average accuracy.
AI.motivation: AI models show inconsistent performance across real-world clinical settings due to variations in patient demographics, imaging protocols, tumor characteristics, and patient subgroups, yet these inconsistencies are poorly quantified at scale.
AI.method: The authors developed an open benchmark of 85,355 CT scans to systematically evaluate 12 tumor-detection AI models across tumor size, location, patient subgroup, and imaging protocol, using LLMs to extract and organize subgroup information from clinical data.
AI.result: Current state-of-the-art AI models optimized for average accuracy perform poorly in rare or underrepresented subgroups (e.g., young, female African Americans), and collecting sufficient annotated data for these rare cases is often impractical.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides a foundation for building more reliable and robust AI models for tumor detection, highlighting the need for rigorous, subgroup-level evaluation in medical imaging and computer vision.

title: DiffusionBench: On Holistic Evaluation of Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion transformer (DiT) research on image generation has converged to a single evaluation setup: class-conditional generation on ImageNet. While methods improve the FID and related metrics, it is increasingly unclear whether they reflect real progress in generative modeling. The natural alternative, i.e., text-to-image (T2I) generation, is perceived as too costly or inconvenient to train and evaluate and is often skipped. We argue that this perception no longer holds. We introduce NanoGen, a unified DiT training and evaluation framework. NanoGen matches state-of-the-art DiT baselines on ImageNet and, with 12 lines of configuration change, also trains competitive text-to-image models. It currently supports RAE, VAE, pixel-space, and MeanFlow diffusion methods under both ImageNet and T2I setups. Under NanoGen, training T2I requires comparable compute to ImageNet. After training 21 latent diffusion models with NanoGen, we observe that method ranking shows no strong correlation between ImageNet and T2I generation: Pearson correlation is between -0.377 and -0.580 across three metrics. This suggests that a method which improves class-conditional ImageNet FID may show no corresponding improvement on T2I, clearly indicating the necessity of evaluating DiTs on both tasks. To this end, we summarize ImageNet and text-to-image results, which yields DiffusionBench, a holistic benchmark for DiT research. We recommend reporting DiffusionBench in place of ImageNet alone: methods that improve DiffusionBench are more likely to reflect broader progress.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NanoGen, a unified DiT training/evaluation framework that enables cost-effective text-to-image (T2I) generation alongside standard ImageNet experiments. After training 21 models, they find no strong correlation between ImageNet and T2I performance (Pearson correlation -0.377 to -0.580), showing that ImageNet improvements don't necessarily transfer to T2I. They propose DiffusionBench as a more holistic benchmark for DiT research.
AI.motivation: Current DiT research over-relies on class-conditional ImageNet evaluation, which may not reflect real progress in generative modeling. The community avoids text-to-image training due to perceived cost/inconvenience, leading to potentially misleading conclusions about method improvements.
AI.method: NanoGen is a unified framework supporting RAE, VAE, pixel-space, and MeanFlow diffusion methods under both ImageNet and T2I setups. It matches SOTA on ImageNet and requires only 12 lines of configuration changes to switch to T2I training, making T2I training comparable in compute to ImageNet training.
AI.result: After training 21 latent diffusion models, Pearson correlation between ImageNet and T2I metrics ranges from -0.377 to -0.580, indicating weak to negative correlation. Methods that improve ImageNet FID may not improve—or may even worsen—T2I performance.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating DiTs solely on ImageNet is insufficient; both ImageNet and T2I evaluations are necessary. The authors recommend reporting DiffusionBench (combined results) as a more meaningful indicator of broader progress in DiT research.
